channel.title: Posts on Random thoughts
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Random thoughts

item.title: The Opacity of the Obvious
item.description:
Previous in the series: The Attractor Hypothesis.

PART I — THE DISTURBANCE

Obviousness Arrives and Erases Its Own History

When I first studied economics, its most basic ideas were, for months, actively counterintuitive. I could follow the definitions, but to work with them I had to hold them in place by force. Then the feeling turned over. The ideas didn’t just come to seem correct; they came to seem obvious, and I lost the ability to recover what had ever been hard about them. The obviousness arrived and erased its own history. I can’t get back into the version of me who found it strange. I can still make the case for it: ask me why this way of modeling an equilibrium is the compelling one and the reasons come readily. But I had those reasons in hand long before they convinced me; I could derive the result while it still felt wrong. What I’ve started to doubt is whether those reasons are what convinced me, or something I assemble now to answer the question. The unsettling part isn’t that I can’t produce reasons. It’s the worry that they may themselves be partly constructed after the fact. The clarity is complete, and there were never reasons in my hand to begin with. There are two things we call obvious. Some obviousness is public and checkable: the truck is bigger than the car; anyone can look. But the kind that matters here is stranger: it feels just as settled, with nothing public to point to.

item.title: The Attractor Hypothesis
item.description:
Previous in the series: The Slopes of Approval.

Next in the series: The Opacity of the Obvious.

What the Pattern Is

Three chapters in, the same shape kept appearing. The chapter on concepts described basins of attraction in a learned representational landscape: sloped sides, fuzzy edges, deeper for prototypes. The chapter on belief recovered the same geometry under a different label. Continuous credences that feel categorical when the system settles. Hysteresis around interpretive switches. Paths into a state mattering for how easily you can leave it. The chapter on moral sentiment did it again. Approval and disapproval as basins. Dilemmas as saddle points where two basins compete. The systematizing impulse of normative theory pushing against terrain that won’t hold neat partitions.

item.title: The Slopes of Approval
item.description:
Previous in the series: The Shape That Belief and Credence Share.

OK, so what should I do?

After the last chapter, I found myself asking a question I hadn’t meant to ask yet.

If belief looks different once we stop treating it as a clean philosophical category and start asking what kind of neural state could produce it, what happens when we do the same thing to morality? Does neurology tell us anything useful about ethical judgment? Not whether an action is right or wrong. That would be much too fast. But does the substrate that produces moral judgment help dissolve some of the tensions that moral philosophy keeps running into, the way it seemed to help in epistemology?

item.title: The Geometry of Meaning
item.description:
Introduction: The physical address of a thought

This whole project started with a book about memory.

I picked up The Forgetting Machine expecting neuroscience. What I got was philosophy.

The chapter that did it was about “concept neurons.” I remember the jolt of realizing that something as abstract as a concept could be tied to a coalition of cells firing in the medial temporal lobe, a memory region deep in the brain. It felt like finding the physical address of a ghost.

item.title: The Shape That Belief and Credence Share
item.description:
Previous in the series: The Geometry of Meaning.

The Wrong Frame

Epistemologists have been having a fight about belief.

On one side, you have the belief-first camp: the traditionalists. They say your mental life is built out of beliefs, discrete, binary commitments, you either believe something or you don’t. You believe it’s raining or you don’t. You believe the earth is round or you don’t. There’s no 0.73 about it. Credences, degrees of confidence, are real enough, but they’re secondary. They ride on top of belief, not the other way around.1

item.title: The I by Reflection
item.description:
Shakespeare wrote “The eye sees not itself / But by reflection.” It’s one of his most profound observations—not just for the poetry, but for what it reveals about understanding itself.

Distance is the prerequisite for insight. Contrast is how we learn.

Here’s what I mean. Think of hearing a recording of your own voice. That shock you feel? It comes from the gap between how you sound to yourself and how you sound to others. You needed that external surface—the recording—to hear what was always there. Without it, you were trapped in the resonance of your own skull.

item.title: When Will Humans Achieve AMI?
item.description:
When Will Humans Achieve AMI?

A Philosophical Dialogue Between Two Primates

Darwin and Wallace sat in their favorite fig tree, watching the strange hairless creatures below.

“I’m telling you,” Wallace said, “they’re getting close to AMI. That one yesterday climbed almost three meters.”

Darwin snorted. “Three meters? My nephew does five before breakfast. And it had to look where to put its hands. No instinct. That’s not AMI.”

The humans huddled around a small fire, making peculiar vocalizations.

item.title: Rate of Progress Part 1: The Value of Intelligence
item.description:
Everyone and their toaster is predicting that AI will change everything. Whole industries vaporized. All jobs obsolete—next week, apparently. You, jobless and serene, writing haikus in a meadow while a bot files your taxes, wins a Pulitzer, and gets optioned for a Netflix series.

The only question: what calendar are these predictors using?
Where does the exuberance come from?

It starts with our overvaluation of intelligence

Intelligence, as it turns out, might be one of the most overrated traits of all time.
It doesn’t make people happier. Or kinder. Or better at relationships.

item.title: It's an Alien Intelligence: Stop Comparing It to a Child
item.description:
We’re obsessed with measuring AI against human children—as if that’s some gold standard for intelligence. It’s like judging a spaceship by how well it floats. Completely absurd.

LLMs aren’t children. They’re not growing up, going to school, or worrying about acne. They aren’t mini-humans waiting to mature. They’re something altogether stranger, an alien intelligence, shaped by statistical patterns instead of evolution, neurons, playground drama, and awkward teenage sex.

Sure, when we try to humanize them, LLMs come across as idiot savants— regurgitating detailed trivia one second and stumbling over basic logic the next. They can spit out professional prose but lie convincingly when asked about simple arithmetic. But measuring them this way is fundamentally misguided. It’s like mocking a dolphin for its inability to ride a bicycle.

item.title: Move 37: How Far Will We Trust AI?
item.description:
Here is another attempt to think about AI more than a few weeks into the future.

I started thinking about this after reading The MANIAC by Benjamín Labatut. In case you’re unfamiliar, John von Neumann was the man behind an astonishingly long list of contributions to humanity, including game theory and the modern computer. If I had met him and we disagreed, I would assume that he knew more, considered more, and analyzed it better than I did—that I was only seeing part of the picture.

Attractor Landscape

Observe how neural fuzziness creates conceptual boundaries.

Concept Network

Concept Neuron

Lateral inhibition forces a single neuron to specialize in related inputs. Red lines show the winning neuron suppressing others.
